coredata.dc:title: Solid-phase microwave synthesis of high-entropy graphene quantum dots as metal-free electrochemical catalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Kuan-Chien
Kumar Panda, Pradeep
Chandra Mallick, Bikash
Yang, Po-Chih
Liu, Wei-Ren
Hsieh, Chien-Te
coredata.dc:description: In this study, high-entropy graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are synthesized and utilized as metal-free electrocatalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in alkaline electrolyte. An efficient solid-state microwave route was used to synthesize the GQD catalysts. This synthesis method involves the pyrolysis of two carbon precursors and four dopant precursors under a pulse microwave irradiation at 180 °C. The synthesized efficient catalyst possesses an average particle size of about 5 nm. The synthesized GQDs catalyst contains six elements in the graphite-like lattices with numerous surface functionalities and dopants. Synthesized high-entropy GQD catalytic electrodes displayed ultra-high electrochemically active surface area and endurance for the ORR measurements. The GQD electrode shows a well-defined reduction peak at ca. −0.5 V and an onset potential at ca. −0.1 V vs. saturated calomel electrode in the diluted potassium hydroxide solution. The heteroatom doping into the GQD nanostructure greatly increased the ORR catalytic activity. The above synthesized non-metallic and inexpensive catalyst showed remarkable properties such as enhanced catalytic activity, selectivity, and superior durability. Therefore, synthesized GQD electrodes have great potential for energy applications, especially for fuel cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
High entropy carbon
Metal-free catalysts
Electrocatalytic activity
Oxygen reduction reaction

ce:italic.ce:bold: SMS of GQD and HEC nanoparticles
Electrochemical activity of GQD and HEC electrodes
Fluorescence and material characterization of GQDs and HECs
Fluorescence properties of the GQDs and HECs
Catalytic activity of GQD and HEC catalysts toward ORR

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) Taiwan, under the contract NSTC 112-2221-E-155-005-MY3.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for illustrating N-doped and HE-GQD samples through an efficient solid-state microwave synthesis route.
(a) XRD patterns of different GQD samples, and (b) survey-scan XPS spectrum of GQD-3 sample.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C1s, (b) O1s, (c) N1s, (d) B1s, (e) S2p, and (f) P2p of GQD-3 sample.
The entropy of mixing of equiatomic ratio alloys as a function of the number of the components in GQDs.
TEM micrographs, SAD patterns, and average particle size of (a, d, g) GQD-1, (b, e, h) GQD-2, and (c, f, i) GQD-3 samples. The inset shows their corresponding lattice fringes.

UV–vis absorbance spectra of (a) GQD-1, (b) GQD-2, and (c) GQD-3 suspension. Photographs of (d) different GQD suspensions emitted by UV irradiation (wavelength: 360 nm) and, (e) GQD inks in paper under visible light and UV illumination (wavelength: 360 nm), where the Chinese character represents “Yuan Ze” University.

PL emission spectra of (a, d) GQD-1, (b, e) GQD-2, and (c, f) GQD-3 suspensions under UV and blue-light illumination. The PL spectra also appear the GQD suspension with and without adding different amounts of paraquat. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

LSV curves of (a) GQD-1, (b) GQD-2, and (c) GQD-3 catalyst electrodes under oxygen atmosphere at different scan rates, (d) Typical chronoamperometry curve of different catalyst electrodes at −0.5 V vs SCE under oxygen atmosphere, and (e) Schematic diagram for describing the oxygen reduction reactions on high-entropy GQD catalysts.
Chemical compositions and lattice parameters of various GQD samples determined by EDS and XRD analyses.

head.ce:title: Solid-phase microwave synthesis of high-entropy graphene quantum dots as metal-free electrochemical catalysts

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Taoyuan 32003, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Taoyuan 32003, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan 32023, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan 32023, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical, Aerospace, and Biomedical Engineering, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN 37996, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical, Aerospace, and Biomedical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical, Aerospace, and Biomedical Engineering, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN 37996, United States

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Kuan-Chien Liu and Pradeep Kumar Panda contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, high-entropy graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are synthesized and utilized as metal-free electrocatalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in alkaline electrolyte. An efficient solid-state microwave route was used to synthesize the GQD catalysts. This synthesis method involves the pyrolysis of two carbon precursors and four dopant precursors under a pulse microwave irradiation at 180 °C. The synthesized efficient catalyst possesses an average particle size of about 5 nm. The synthesized GQDs catalyst contains six elements in the graphite-like lattices with numerous surface functionalities and dopants. Synthesized high-entropy GQD catalytic electrodes displayed ultra-high electrochemically active surface area and endurance for the ORR measurements. The GQD electrode shows a well-defined reduction peak at ca. −0.5 V and an onset potential at ca. −0.1 V vs. saturated calomel electrode in the diluted potassium hydroxide solution. The heteroatom doping into the GQD nanostructure greatly increased the ORR catalytic activity. The above synthesized non-metallic and inexpensive catalyst showed remarkable properties such as enhanced catalytic activity, selectivity, and superior durability. Therefore, synthesized GQD electrodes have great potential for energy applications, especially for fuel cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fuel cells (FCs) have received a lot of interest aspromising electrochemical energy transfer technology owing to their low emissions and outstanding energy efficiency [1–4]. The catalyst is the one major component in the FCs, which is mainly responsible for the performance of FCs and overall cost. Particularly, precious metals (such as platinum (Pt)) exhibit outstanding catalytic activity towards hydrogen desorption/adsorption and methanol oxidation phenomenon [5,6]. However, the electrochemical performance, especially the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) of FCs (e.g., proton exchange membrane of FCs and metal-air batteries) is hindered due to the ORR sluggish reaction kinetics [7–9]. Furthermore, it is also well known that precious metal-based alloys (such as Pt − M catalysts such as M = Fe [10], Co [11–16], Ni [17,18], Sn [19–21], and Ag [22]) commonly utilized for the ORR catalytic activity, that typically exhibits high current density through four-electron pathways. However, such Pt-based electrocatalysts exhibited poor stability, CO poisoning, and deactivation by methanol. Thus, developing a suitable catalyst for the ORR is crucial, which should be both extremely effective and affordable.

For the ORR catalytic activity, prior attempts have been made to develop the suitable electrodes based on non-precious metals [23–27] and metal-free catalysts [28–31]. According to the published reports, heteroatom doped carbon-based materials exhibited enhanced electrocatalytic activity as well as high stability [32–34] for the ORR. Heteroatoms such as N, S, and B, have been doped into the carbon materials on a large scale to change the electrical/chemical characteristics by designing the morphology's electronic structure. Thus, nanocarbon materials doped with heteroatoms are intriguing metal-free catalysts [9,35–37]. Recently, all researchers have widely investigated high-entropy alloys (HEAs) multifunctional materials in a solid solution phase due to their tailored magnetic, electrical, thermal, and catalytic characteristics [38–40]. The HEAs have been primarily utilized in the electrocatalysis of ORR [41], carbon dioxide [42], methanol [39,43], and hydrogen evolution reactions [40]. These multicomponent structures within a simple lattice (e.g., (face-centered cubic (FCC) and body-centered cubic (BCC)) can be utilized for various catalytic applications. Indeed, preparing HEA containing catalytically active materials has enabled single-atom catalysts [44]. Despite being extremely promising, only a few prior research has been attempted on the production of high-entropy carbon (HEC) nanodots as FCs electrocatalysts.

HEAs are typically single-phase solid solutions. It contains at least five principal elements [45,46]. The multi-element nature of these HEAs imparts high mixing entropy, while assisting in the development of stable solid solutions in simple crystal structures (e.g., FCC and BCC) [47]. Accordingly, in this work, we have employed two kinds of carbon sources (i.e., citric acid and fructose) and four dopants (i.e., N, B, S, and P) for growing well-mixed solid solution through an efficient solid-state microwave synthesis (SMS) route. The microwave-assisted approach is an ultra-efficient method with excellent uniformity and simplicity in execution [48,49]. Indeed, the SMS approach is capable of synthesizing HEC electrocatalytic nanoparticles for ORR and the electro-oxidation of carbon dioxide. Here, we have also systematically explored the as-synthesized HECs as metal-free catalysts for ORR in KOH electrolytes. The findings of this work give new techniques for developing HEC as a metal-free catalyst for FCs, which is both highly efficient and robust.

ce:italic.ce:bold: SMS of GQD and HEC nanoparticles

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized using the microwave-assisted technique as per our previous studies [48,49]. Herein, we employed the SMS technique for synthesizing different GQDs under a pulse microwave irradiation. In order to synthesize N-doped GQDs, a high-performance homogenizer and a 2D mixer (with Zr balls) were used to uniformly mix 25 mL of distilled water with three precursors such as citric acid (1.92 g, C6H8O7, molecular weight: 192.12 g/mol, purity: 99 %), fructose (1.8 g, C6H12O6, molecular weight: 180, purity: 99 %), and urea (1.8 g, CON2H4, molecular weight: 60, purity: 99 %) at 300 rpm for 5 min. The above homogenous solid mixtures (10 g) were placed in a microwave oven (960 W and 2.45 GHz). The microwave oven was well equipped with a K-type thermocouple and a temperature controller. Thereafter, the maximum temperature (180 °C), and the pulse microwave parameters (total cycle number: 200 cycles, power-on time: 3 s, and power-off time: 3 s) were adjusted. The resultant product (N-doped GQD) was naturally cooled to room temperature. Then, N-doped GQD were ball-milled moderately and sieved (200-mesh stainless steel foil mesh). Finally, the sample was thoroughly dispersed in pure distilled water and centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm.

To prepare the S-/N-codoped GQDs, the precursors with various carbon sources (1.92 g of citric acid and 1.8 g of fructose) and dopant sources (1.8 g of urea and 0.396 g of ammonium sulfate) were utilized. Similar precursors (e.g., 1.92 g of citric acid, 1.8 g of fructose, 1.8 g of urea, 0.396 g of ammonium sulfate, 0.193 g of boric acid, and 0.246 g of phosphorous acid) were employed for growing the HEC nanoparticles. Two types of GQDs were synthesized by the SMS method under an identical operating condition. GQD-1, GQD-2, and GQD-3 were assigned to as-prepared N-doped GQD, S-/N-codoped GQD, and HEC nanoparticles. The preparation method of N-doped and high-entropy GQDs were provided in the schematic diagram (Fig. 1 ).

ce:italic.ce:bold: Electrochemical activity of GQD and HEC electrodes

ce:section.ce:para: For conducting the electrochemical measurements, carbon paper (CP, Toray Composites Inc.) was purchased for the preparation of the electrode. Here, CP acts as a substrate, which consists of a series of carbon fibers with an average diameter about of 8–10 μm, arrayed in an irregular configuration. The electrodes were fabricated by depositing synthesized GQD and HEC nanoparticles onto carbon paper. First, the slurry was prepared for the electrodes. For the electrode slurry preparation, first prepare a mixture of a solution, which contains distilled water (30 mL) and Nafion® (DuPontTM, USA) 117 (3 mL) (Sigma Aldrich; 5 vol%). Then, both GQD and HEC powders (100 mg) were well dispersed into the above solution and sonication an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. The above-ultrasonicated slurry was deposited onto CP (2 cm × 1 cm) using a sequential dipping layer-by-layer method. Finally, the electrode was dehydrated in a vacuum oven overnight at 80 °C.

A three-electrode cell setup was used to investigate the electrochemical properties of the prepared electrodes. Here, the above-prepared electrode act as a working electrode, while Pt wire, and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) serve as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The working electrode is immersed fully into the electrolyte. The samples were put on a stainless-steel foil that served as the current collector to synthesize the catalytic electrodes. The ORR activity of GQD and HEC catalysts was evaluated from a linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). For the LSV, an alkaline electrolyte (0.1 M KOH) was used. During the analysis, oxygen was continuously flowing at the rate of 100 mL min−1. Prior to analysis, nitrogen gas (N2, 99.99 %) was supplied for 30 min. Typical chronoamperometry of the catalytic electrodes was carried out at −0.5 V vs. SCE under an O2 atmosphere. Room temperature was used for all electrochemical measurements.

ce:italic.ce:bold: Fluorescence and material characterization of GQDs and HECs

ce:section.ce:para: The atomic ratio in both GQD and HEA catalysts was determined using energy diffraction spectroscopy (EDS). The structural morphology of the samples was assessed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL, JEM-210). Absorbance spectra of samples were determined through an ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrometer (Agilent Technology Cary 60). For the UV–Vis spectra, samples (200 mg/L) were taken in quartz cells, and 60 nm min−1 scan rate was applied. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were collected at 360 nm using the fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000 FLS920P). For PL, samples were prepared at 200 mg mL−1 concentration. The crystallinity of the carbon samples was determined using an X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu Labx XRD-6000), which is equipped with a Cu–Kα emitter. The bonding structure was identified through Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw Micro-Raman spectrometer). The exact chemical composition was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fison VG ESCA210). The deconvolution was done for the detected elements (C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, B 1s, S 2p, and P 2p) through XPS. The deconvolution was performed using a non-linear least squares fitting algorithm developed in-house based on the symmetric Gaussian function.

To determine the chemical composition of as-prepared electrodes, the EDS was adopted, and the atomic ratios in GQD and HEC catalysts were quantified, as shown in Table 1 . Based on Table 1, the GQD-1 sample contains mainly C, N, and O elements, while the GQD-2 electrode consists of N, O, C, and S. The appearance of the element S (i.e., 2.9 at.%) is due to the addition of ammonium sulfate into the precursor. As expected, more than six elements can be found within the GQD-3 sample, forming a high-entropy GQD (i.e., the HEC nanoparticles: C33N27O22S2B15P2).

The XRD pattern gives the crucial information about the crystallinity structure. The XRD pattern of GQD and HEC are displayed in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2, one typical d (002) significant peak is detected at 2-theta range around 24.9°–26.2° (JCPDS Card No.: 41–1487), indicating the existence of a crystalline graphite-like structure. The interlayer spacing is calculated using the Bragg’s Law. The interlayer spacing distance of the GQD-1 sample is ∼ 0.340 nm, equivalent to the interlayer spacing distance of a perfect graphite (0.335–0.340 nm) [50]. In contrast, the interlayer spacing distance of the GQD-2 and GQD-3 samples shift to 0.356 and 0.357 nm, respectively. The shifting of interlayer distance is mainly due to the doping of heteroatoms. When heteroatoms are doped successfully, the graphene layer disturbed in the graphite lattice, and as a result interlayer distance increase. Further, the mean crystalline size (crystalline parameter) along the c-axis (L C), may also be estimated from the peak width determined at the coordinate corresponding with the half-maximal d (002) peak. The L C for the GQD-1 sample was calculated using the Debye-Scherrer’s equation, to be about 0.885 nm, however, the L C values of the GQD-2 and GQD-3 samples were substantially lower i.e., 0.577 and 0.580 nm, respectively. As compared to that of the GQD-1 sample, reduced L C values mainly originate from the replacement of sp2 hybridization structure and oxygen/nitrogen functionalities (e.g., carboxylic and amino groups) by heteroatoms, that result in creating a number of cavities, broken bonds, and voids in the GQD lattices. The reduced crystallite size is a result of discrete graphene defects that appeared in the HEC samples [51].

Fig. 2(b) displayed the XPS full scan spectra of the GQD-3 sample. The displayed full XPS spectra have six peaks at ca. 532, 400, 284, 191, 167, and 133 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s, B1s, S2p, and P2p, atoms respectively. To determine the various types of C-bonding, C1s spectrum of the GQD-3 sample was deconvoluted into five peaks with centers at ca. 285.7 eV (CN/CB/CS), 284.5 eV (CC/CC), 288.6 eV (CO), 286.8 eV (COH), and 289.8 eV (OCO) [52] (see Fig. 3 (a)). Further, oxygen functionalities are confirmed in Fig. 3(b). The majority of the oxygen functional groups in sp2 graphitic-like lattices are linked to the edge (e.g., carboxylic and phenolic group) or on the basal plane (e.g., epoxy or carbonyl group) of the lattice due to their high O/C atomic ratio (i.e., high oxidation level). The deconvoluted N1s spectrum is presented in Fig. 3(c), which indicates the presence of pyrrolic/pyridinic N (ca. 399.6 eV) and quaternary N (ca. 400.2 eV) [53,54]. This finding indicates that the majority of the pyridinic N (∼89 %) is bound within the lattice, mostly present at the edge sites rather than the basal plane sites [55]. Fig. 3(d) displayed the B1s spectrum of the GQD-3 sample, which contains three main compounds such as BC3 (ca. 189.3 eV), BC2O (ca. 190.8 eV), and BCO2 (ca. 193.6 eV) [56,57]. It can be concluded that BC3 and BC2O bonding types are responsible for the formation of the B dopants on the GQD skeleton configuration. The high resolution of the S2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 3(e)) clearly displays three peaks at around 163.8, 170.8 eV, and 167.0, which correspond to CSC, CSO4 and CSOx bonding, respectively [56,57]. As a result, there are two main configurations of doped S, i.e., oxide-S and thiophene sulphur [58]. Fig. 3(f) presents the P2p spectrum of the GQD-3 sample, which contains three major components with binding energies of ca. 132.2, 133.5, and 135.8 eV, with relative percentages of 5.9, 83.2, and 10.9 %, respectively. The peak at ca. 132.2 (lower binding energy), 133.5 (higher binding energy), and 135.8 eV (higher binding energy) were corresponded to the P − CCC, phosphonate/phosphate and CP groups, respectively [59]. Based on the XPS analysis, the coexistence of various types of C, O, N, B, S, and P functionalities within the HEC lattices suggests the formation of a high-mixing solid solution. Further, all of the atoms in GQD-1 (C1s, O1s, and N1s) and GQD-2 (C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p) samples were deconvoluted and shown in Figs. S1 and S2, respectively, in the supplemental section. The above results proved that all the samples were synthesized successfully.

Yeh et al. devised one of the first models for the random mixing of components that accounted for the high entropy effect [60]. The entropy of mixing (ΔS mix) can be determined using this equation ΔS mix = R ln (n), where n is the equal molar concentration of each element. This equation can be used to describe an n-element solution phase [45,61]. Fig. 4 shows the entropy for mixing GQDs with equal atomic ratios as a function of the number of components (n) in the GQD lattice. Herein, the heterogeneous GQDs altered by the number of components can be divided into three regions: low entropy (n < 2, ΔS mix < 0.69R), high entropy GQDs (n > 5, ΔS mix > 1.61 R), and medium entropy (2 < n < 5, 0.69 R < ΔS mix < 1.61 R). This correlation between ΔS mix and n number (#) can be used to analyze the intrinsic properties of the nanostructured carbon materials; however, the existence and feasibility of HECs with different chemical compositions still require an in-depth investigation and high-level computational studies (e.g., density functional theory (DFT) calculation).

It has already been shown that single-phase HEC can successfully form due to the entropy contribution to the reduction of the total free energy of the system [62,63]. The Gibbs free energy is highly dependent upon the formation of crystalline structure. Moreover, Gibbs free energy (ΔG) is associated with the formation of the crystalline structure from the thermodynamic point of view. As per the proposed equation, ΔG is highly dependent upon the temperature. HEC nanoparticles Gibbs free energy can be expressed as: ΔG = ΔH mix – TΔS mix [64]. In the SMS pathway, the highly localized large-scale energy release under pulsed microwave irradiation in the instantaneous lifetime dominates the reaction direction compared to the enthalpy contribution. Therefore, low-temperature SMS synthesis of nanocrystals is possible [64]. In high-entropy systems, the TΔSmix factor increases excessively with increasing temperature, and thus may exceed ΔHmix and contribute to single-phase stabilization. Therefore, the high configurational entropy minimizes the total free energy of single phases and prevents the formation of other crystalline phases. Further, to investigate the uniform distribution of GQDs and HECs, low and high-magnification TEM images are taken, and displayed in Fig. 5 (a)–5(c). All the samples exhibited a spherical-like shape with an average particle size of ∼ 5 nm (Fig. 5(g)–(i)). The inset of Fig. 5 illustrates the lattice fringes in each individual particle, demonstrating the significant influence of the dopant number on the tortuosity within the lattices. Fig. 5 shows that the GQD-1 sample has a multi-layered crystallinity that corresponds to the d 1100 lattice fringes of the graphene sheets and has a lattice distance of 0.21 nm [65]. Increasing the surface heterogeneity, the heteroatom doping induces a low crystallinity HECs (i.e., GQD-3), and decreases the L C value, while imparting a higher deviation percentage of the lattice constant (see Table 1) due to the disorderness of heavy atom doping. Indeed, heavy doping level results in a large number of heteroatoms and disrupts the conjugated sp2 cluster. Additionally, it increases the density of point-like defects through formation of the disordered structures (such as tortuous lattices) [66]. The analysis of the XRD patterns also confirmed the same observation.

Moreover, selected-area diffraction (SAD) patterns of the samples were also recorded, as shown in Fig. 5(d)–(f)). It is important to note that the electrons passing through different graphene domains are diffracted at various azimuthal angles. Therefore, the diffracted pattern is constructed to overlay of each graphene domain. Herein, the SAD pattern of the GQD-1 sample contains several rings, indicating the presence of the poly-crystalline structure as well as a relatively large crystalline morphology [67]. Contrarily, the SAD of the GQD-3 sample exhibits a large number of irregular diffraction spots as well as diffraction rings, indicating the presence of lattice deformation and broken bonding inside the HEC grain.

ce:italic.ce:bold: Fluorescence properties of the GQDs and HECs

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 (a)–(c) displayed the UV–vis absorbance spectra of the GQD and HEC suspensions. All the spectra were acquired within 200–800 nm wavelength range and the sample concentration were taken as 100 mg/L. All sample’s spectra show a typical π–π* transition absorption peak at ca. 260 nm. This peak originated due to aromatic sp2 domains (CC). Another typical absorption peak appeared at ca. 340 nm, which corresponds to CO/CN bonding. This peak appeared due to the n–π* transition. Another notable peak appeared at 410 nm, which originates due to n–π* transitions of the nonbonding electrons of the adatoms. This peak is the band gap transition absorption band and it extend into visible range with a long tail [68,69]. However, a pronounced tail effect is identified in GQD-3 suspension sample, which dominates the n–π* transitions at ∼ 410 nm (Fig. 6c). This tailing effect occurred due to the formation of heavy adatom doping in the absorption bands, which means the wavelength region of 550–595 nm due to the π–π* and n–π* of CS and SO bonds, respectively [70]. To investigate the fluorescent properties of GQDs and HECs, the prepared suspensions were exposed to a UV light at a wavelength of 360 nm, as shown in Fig. 6(d). Interestingly, allsamples produce blue light, when exposed to UV light due to the substantial adatom doping,with the little variance in the spectral signal. Accordingly, the GQD-3 suspension was prepared as a calligraphy ink and the Chinese characters, “Yuan Ze” University, were successfully written down on a piece of paper (see Fig. 6(e)). These Chinese characters successfully emit blue fluorescence under UV irradiation.

Fig. 7 shows GQD and HEC suspensions’ PL emission spectrum. Distilled water was used for the preparation of GQD and HEC suspensions. The emission spectra were taken at 360 and 450 nm excited wavelengths. Under the UV irradiation, all the samples’ suspensions exhibit a quasi-symmetric peak, while the samples GQD-1, GQD-2, and GQD-3 exhibit maximal peaks appeared at ca. 420, 432, and 440 nm, respectively. This result clearly indicates that the red-shift characteristic was identified due to a higher amount of dopant concentration, which alters the optical band gap. As for the blue-light illumination, all the samples still exhibit a quasi-symmetric PL peak along with a minor red-shift within the spectra. The maximal intensities appear at ca. 538, 540, and 543 nm for the GQD-1, GQD-2, and GQD-3 samples, respectively. This PL variation within the optical bandgap is mainly due to the multiple chromophoric bandgap structure, which is raised from the gain boundary doped defect emission traps.

The PL response spectra of GQD suspensions with different paraquat concentrations excited at 360 nm and 450 nm are also shown in Fig. 7. From Fig. 7, it is found that PL intensity decreases with increasing paraquat concentration. The PL quenching was shown due to the presence of paraquat. However, it is important to note that the change in PL intensity in GQD-3 suspensions with paraquat concentration was more pronounced compared to GQD-1 and GQD-2 samples. This indicates that GQD-3 suspensions can be considered as efficient sensor probes for detecting specific toxic compounds in liquid phases. PL quenching of HEC suspensions in the presence of paraquat is attributed to the fact that the majority of HEC–(paraquat) x intermediates exhibit aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) due to strong intermolecular π-π stacking [70]. In fact, HEC has a super hydrophilic surface due to the presence of functional groups of oxygen, nitrogen, and boron. The ACQ effect is greatly enhanced with increasing HEC aggregation, eventually resulting in the quenching of fluorescence emission with increasing paraquat concentration. Such sensitive responses indicate that HEC is a very promising sensory nanomaterial.

ce:italic.ce:bold: Catalytic activity of GQD and HEC catalysts toward ORR

ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the ORR activity, LSV measurements were done. LSV is a useful approach for studying the electrochemical behavior of materials that involves sweeping the potential applied to an electrode or electrolyte at a constant rate [71]. In this analysis, on the synthesized electrodes were used and 0.1 M KOH was taken as an electrolyte. The various sweep rates (30, 50, 70, and 90 mV s−1) were set up and during analysis oxygen gas was used; the gas flow rate was at 100 mL min−1. The obtained data is presented in Fig. 8 (a)–(c). The maximum current at 90 mV s−1 throughout the whole potential window showed the following trends: GQD-3 (1120 mA g−1) > GQD-2 (630 mA g−1) > GQD-3 (270 mA g−1). This comparison results agree well with the corresponding onset potentials. The ORR activities were improved due to the introduction of heavy atom doping, leading to a significant increase in borization and amidation levels in HEC. Accordingly, the GQD-3 catalytic electrode enables the highest ORR activity, as compared to other samples. The ORR mechanism of the HEC catalysts in KOH electrolyte can be written as follows [30]:
(R1) HEC* + O2 → HEC*−O2(ads)
(R2) HEC*−O2(ads) + 2 H2O + 4 e− → HEC* + 4 OH−
(R3) HEC*−O2(ads) + 2 H2O + 2 e− → HEC*–HO2 − (ads) + OH−
(R4) HEC*–HO2 − (ads) + H2O + 2 e− → HEC* + 3 OH−

Here, the HEC* represents the active sites available for the adsorption/reduction of oxygen. The ORR catalytic cycle of the HEC catalytic electrodes consists of (i) chemisorption of O2 (R1), (ii) four-electron charge transfer steps (R2), and (iii) two-electron charge transfer steps (R3 and R4). During the chemisorption, HECs provide many active sites (i.e., defects and dangling bonds), that allow the formation of the HEC*−O2(ads) intermediates. Thus, O2-occupied sites increased, which facilates the progress of subsequent R2, R3, and R4 steps.

The pronounced ORR activity observed in HEC catalysts can be attributed to the increased chemisorption sites for molecular oxygen and the superior catalytic ability enabled by the four-electron (R2-R4) transfer pathway. From the DFT calculations, BC3 plays a crucial role in influencing the electronic transition, while the effect of the BC2O and BCO2 (oxidized B bonding configurations) on the electronic characteristics of HECs, which is determined by the carbon’s hybridization form of carbon [72]. B atom have less electronegativity than C; therefore, B atoms can incorporate with C atoms easily. This electronegativity phenomenon occurred due to oxygen molecule adsorbed on the B atoms, as a result, O2 reduction occurs [9]. In other words, during the catalytic cycle, molecular oxygen interacts with the B center, and the B center’s electronic properties drive catalytic activity.

The DFT study further indicated that doped graphene’s P atoms split H2O through an intermediate with a P − OH group and a H atom, which are attached to adjacent C atoms [59]. This dynamic interaction results from hydrolysis and refreshing the catalytic sites, enabling the progress of (R2)–(R4). Considering the S dopant in HECs, the thiophene S has been demonstrated to act as an active site in facilitating the catalytic activity toward the ORR [73]. The enhanced ORR activity on the N-doped carbon catalysts can also be observed using DFT analyses. Here, the increased binding energy of oxygen atoms at multiple N-doped graphene centers enables a four-electron ORR pathway (i.e. R2) [74]. The ORR cycle is initiated due to adsorbing the oxygen molecules through carbon atoms. Here, the carbon atom adjacent to the pyridinic N with Lewis act as an active site [75]. On the other hand, the pyridinic N sites (N atoms with a lone pair electrons) also promote the ORR activity by delocalizing the π-electron from the pyridinic N-atom [76]. The doped nitrogen with an OCN edge structure have potential to catalyze the ORR in a two-electron pathway (R3 and R4) [77]. In this study, the enhanced catalytic activity of HEC is responsible for the synergistic effect of the dopants (B, S, P, and N) as shown in Fig. 8(e).

To confirm the anti-poisoning ability of the samples as prepared, chronoamperometric tests were performed at a constant potential of − 0.5 V vs. SCE in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte, as shown in Fig. 8(d). As shown in Fig. 8(d), the ORR current slightly decreases within the first few minutes due to the formation of adsorbed intermediates such as adsorbed species (e.g., GQD–CO(ads)). Indeed, this high initial current indicates that a large number of active sites are available for adsorption and surface reactions [78]. After the initial decay, the current profiles stabilized for all the samples. Notably, the GQD-3 catalyst exhibits the highest current plateau, indicating an increase in catalytically active surface area along with improved CO tolerance. This observation supports increased catalytic activity along with long-term anti-poisoning effects made possible by the design of the HEC catalytic electrode, where the structural motif more efficiently reduces the number of CO(ads) sites and improves CO tolerance.

In summary, a pulse microwave-assisted technique was employed for synthesizing the high-entropy GQD catalysts at 180 °C. The synthesized GQDs possessed an average particle sizeca. 5 nm with six elements, which are graphite-like lattices enabling many surface functionalities along with the dopants. The high-entropy GQD suspensions were able to emit high fluorescence, while serving as ultra-sensitive probes in detecting specific toxic compounds (e.g., paraquat). The GQD catalytic electrodes demonstrated exceptional ORR capacity in alkaline electrolytes, with an onset potential of around −0.1 V, and a clearly defined reduction peak at ca. −0.5 V vs. SCE. The ORR mechanism was well proposed in the alkaline solutions. This sequential reaction mechanism, including the chemisorption of O2, four-electron, and two-electron charge transfer steps. The ORR was indeed significantly enhanced using as-synthesized GQD nanomaterials. The prepared electrodes are inexpensive, with high catalytic activity, superior durability, and selectivity. Above finding suggested that the developed high-entropy GQD catalytic electrodes have great potential for the field of energy applications.

Kuan-Chien Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Software, Methodology. Pradeep Kumar Panda: Investigation, Formal analysis, Software, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Bikash Chandra Mallick: Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Po-Chih Yang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Wei-Ren Liu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Chien-Te Hsieh: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) Taiwan, under the contract NSTC 112-2221-E-155-005-MY3.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.159061.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
